title: Your Life as a Samurai at Every Level (You End With Nothing)
hook: You're nine, barefoot in a rice paddy, and all you want are the two swords on the old warrior's hip. Sixty years later you kneel on a white mat — a lord who owns nothing.
body: A cinematic POV climb through every rank of feudal Japan — you are a nameless peasant boy who rises to lord and lives to watch the sword class abolished. You live it in second person, present tense, level by level: the ashigaru foot-soldier handed a borrowed bamboo spear and shoved into the front rank → the samurai granted the daishō, the two swords, a surname, and a stipend of thirty koku → the mounted retainer with five hundred koku, a horse and men who die on your word → the karō who runs the whole domain → the daimyō who crosses the 10,000-koku line into true lordship → the great lord of a million-koku Kaga-scale domain → and the level above the sword itself: the merchant with no blade who holds your debt. Your rank, your pay, your lord's power — all measured in KOKU, the rice to feed one person for one year, and the number climbs on screen the whole way up. Real mechanics, dramatized: the daishō and who may wear two swords, Hideyoshi's 1588 sword hunt that sealed the class line forever, bushidō and seppuku and the kaishakunin who ends your pain with one cut, sankin-kōtai (alternate attendance) that kept lords drained and their families hostage in Edo, the chōnin merchant class ranked legally last yet holding every daimyō's debt, the Dōjima Rice Exchange of 1730 — one of the world's first futures markets, pricing the samurai's own pay — and the end of it all: the Meiji Restoration, stipends commuted to paper bonds in 1876, the Haitōrei edict banning the sword, and the Satsuma Rebellion of 1877 where the last samurai were beaten by a conscript army of peasants with rifles. The twist the whole video is built on: the sword was never the wealth. Rice was. Then paper was. Watch the whole series — every life, every level. Dramatization for educational and entertainment purposes; the Arima clan is fictional, the mechanics are real.
